title: Validation scope and theoretical sources
text: multicentre data reproduce multilocation repeated-measures example Section 28.3 Milliken Johnson (2009). data contain 153 rows, including 28 missing responses, reference model uses 125 observations. Milliken, G. ., Johnson, D. E. (2009). Analysis Messy Data, Volume 1: Designed Experiments (2nd ed.). Chapman Hall/CRC. https://doi.org/10.1201/EBK1584883340

title: Validation scope and theoretical sources
text: active tests compare covariance parameters, fixed effects, type III tests, LS-means, pairwise differences, rowwise Satterthwaite degrees freedom stored SAS PROC MIXED benchmarks specification. exact SAS statements given validation vignette. PROC MIXED algorithms syntax documented current SAS/STAT User’s Guide: https://documentation.sas.com/doc/en/statug/latest/statug_mixed_syntax01.htm. Estimates standard errors tested absolute tolerance 1e-3. Degrees freedom compared precision stored benchmark. Overlapping model classes independently compared nlme, lmerTest, mmrm.
code: fit <- lmm(   multicentre,   Y ~ Drug * Time,   random = ~ 1 | Center,   repeated = ~ Time | Center:Drug:Subject,   structure = "ar1",   method = "REML",   ddf = "satterthwaite" )

title: Validation scope and theoretical sources
text: implementation grounded following primary references: Patterson, H. D., Thompson, R. (1971), restricted maximum likelihood. https://doi.org/10.1093/biomet/58.3.545 Harville, D. . (1977), ML REML variance-component estimation mixed-model prediction. https://doi.org/10.1080/01621459.1977.10480998 LaMotte, L. R. (2007), direct derivation REML likelihood. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00362-006-0335-6 Satterthwaite, F. E. (1946), approximate denominator degrees freedom. https://doi.org/10.2307/3002019 Fai, . H.-T., Cornelius, P. L. (1996), multi-degree--freedom approximate F-tests. https://doi.org/10.1080/00949659608811740 Searle, S. R., Speed, F. M., Milliken, G. . (1980), population marginal means. https://doi.org/10.1080/00031305.1980.10483031 Pinheiro, J. C., Bates, D. M. (2000), structured covariance models. https://doi.org/10.1007/b98882

what: A model with two covariance sources
title: Getting started with lmmix
text: example data contain random center effect repeated observations within subjects. summary contains coefficient-level tests, type III tests, covariance parameters, information criteria.
code: fit <- lmm(   multicentre,   Y ~ Drug * Time,   random = ~ 1 | Center,   repeated = ~ Time | Center:Drug:Subject,   structure = "ar1" )  fit #> Linear mixed model fit by REML #> Formula: `Y ~ Drug * Time` #> Random: `~1 | Center` #> Repeated: `~Time | Center:Drug:Subject` (AR1) #> Log-likelihood: -245.313 #> Convergence code: 0 summary(fit) #>  #> ── Linear mixed model ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Estimation: REML #> Denominator df: satterthwaite #> Residual covariance: AR1 #>  #> ── Fixed effects ── #>  #> # A tibble: 9 × 8 #>   term        estimate std.error statistic    df     p.value conf.low conf.high #>   <chr>          <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl>       <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 (Intercept)   12.1       1.54      7.83   3.06 0.00405        7.21     16.9   #> 2 Drug2          4.76      1.12      4.25  49.8  0.0000928      2.51      7.02  #> 3 Drug3          3.94      1.12      3.52  49.8  0.000939       1.69      6.19  #> 4 Time2          0.921     0.294     3.13  67.5  0.00255        0.335     1.51  #> 5 Time3          2.39      0.441     5.42  72.1  0.000000750    1.51      3.27  #> 6 Drug2:Time2   -0.143     0.438    -0.326 67.9  0.746         -1.02      0.732 #> 7 Drug3:Time2   -0.474     0.448    -1.06  68.0  0.294         -1.37      0.421 #> 8 Drug2:Time3    0.823     0.666     1.24  72.7  0.221         -0.505     2.15  #> 9 Drug3:Time3    0.334     0.644     0.519 73.1  0.605         -0.949     1.62 #> ── Type III tests ── #> # A tibble: 3 × 5 #>   term      num.df den.df statistic  p.value #>   <chr>      <dbl>  <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 Drug           2   46.4     11.4  9.23e- 5 #> 2 Time           2   68.5     59.3  1.16e-15 #> 3 Drug:Time      4   68.4      1.35 2.60e- 1 #> ── Covariance parameters ── #> # A tibble: 3 × 5 #>   group    term        component estimate std.error #>   <chr>    <chr>       <chr>        <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 Center   (Intercept) var          5.17     5.73   #> 2 Residual ar1         var         10.7      2.14   #> 3 Residual ar1         cor          0.935    0.0169 #> ── Information criteria ── #>    logLik       AIC       BIC  deviance  #> -245.3129  514.6257  548.5655  490.6257

title: Getting started with lmmix
text: Nuisance-factor levels receive equal weights. Numeric covariates requested specs held observed means. construction targets population marginal means sense Searle et al. (1980).
code: lsmeans(fit, ~Drug) #> # A tibble: 3 × 8 #>   Drug  estimate std.error    df statistic p.value conf.low conf.high #>   <fct>    <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 1         13.2      1.53  2.98      8.60 0.00339     8.27      18.0 #> 2 2         18.1      1.53  3.01     11.8  0.00128    13.3       23.0 #> 3 3         17.1      1.53  3.01     11.1  0.00154    12.2       21.9 lsmeans(fit, pairwise ~ Drug) #> ── Estimated marginal means ── #>  #> # A tibble: 3 × 8 #>   Drug  estimate std.error    df statistic p.value conf.low conf.high #>   <fct>    <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 1         13.2      1.53  2.98      8.60 0.00339     8.27      18.0 #> 2 2         18.1      1.53  3.01     11.8  0.00128    13.3       23.0 #> 3 3         17.1      1.53  3.01     11.1  0.00154    12.2       21.9 #> ── Pairwise contrasts ── #> # A tibble: 3 × 8 #>   contrast estimate std.error    df statistic   p.value conf.low conf.high #>   <chr>       <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 1 - 2       -4.99      1.10  46.1    -4.54  0.0000400    -7.20     -2.78 #> 2 1 - 3       -3.89      1.10  46.1    -3.54  0.000914     -6.11     -1.68 #> 3 2 - 3        1.10      1.10  46.9     0.993 0.326        -1.12      3.32

title: Getting started with lmmix
text: emmeans installed, package supplies required basis uses model’s Satterthwaite degrees freedom.
code: generics::tidy(fit) #> # A tibble: 9 × 7 #>   effect term        estimate std.error statistic    df     p.value #>   <chr>  <chr>          <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl>       <dbl> #> 1 fixed  (Intercept)   12.1       1.54      7.83   3.06 0.00405     #> 2 fixed  Drug2          4.76      1.12      4.25  49.8  0.0000928   #> 3 fixed  Drug3          3.94      1.12      3.52  49.8  0.000939    #> 4 fixed  Time2          0.921     0.294     3.13  67.5  0.00255     #> 5 fixed  Time3          2.39      0.441     5.42  72.1  0.000000750 #> 6 fixed  Drug2:Time2   -0.143     0.438    -0.326 67.9  0.746       #> 7 fixed  Drug3:Time2   -0.474     0.448    -1.06  68.0  0.294       #> 8 fixed  Drug2:Time3    0.823     0.666     1.24  72.7  0.221       #> 9 fixed  Drug3:Time3    0.334     0.644     0.519 73.1  0.605 generics::glance(fit) #> # A tibble: 1 × 8 #>   logLik   AIC   BIC deviance    df  nobs convergence method #>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl> <int> <int>       <int> <chr>  #> 1  -245.  515.  549.     491.    12   125           0 REML head(generics::augment(fit)) #> # A tibble: 6 × 8 #>   Center Drug  Subject Time      Y .fitted .resid .std.resid #>   <fct>  <fct> <fct>   <fct> <dbl>   <dbl>  <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 R      1     1       1        17    13.0  4.05      1.24   #> 2 R      1     2       1        12    13.0 -0.954    -0.292  #> 3 R      1     2       2        14    13.9  0.125     0.0383 #> 4 R      1     2       3        15    15.3 -0.346    -0.106  #> 5 R      1     3       1        12    13.0 -0.954    -0.292  #> 6 R      1     3       2        11    13.9 -2.87     -0.880 emmeans::emmeans(fit, ~Drug) #> NOTE: Results may be misleading due to involvement in interactions #>  Drug emmean   SE   df lower.CL upper.CL #>  1      13.2 1.53 2.98     8.27     18.0 #>  2      18.1 1.53 3.01    13.28     23.0 #>  3      17.1 1.53 3.01    12.18     21.9 #>  #> Results are averaged over the levels of: Time  #> Confidence level used: 0.95

title: Multi-site PROC MIXED validation workflow
text: multicentre data reproduce multilocation repeated-measures example described Milliken Johnson (2009) Section 28.3. design three centers, three drugs, three measurement times, 153 rows. 28 missing responses, fitted model uses 125 complete responses.

title: Multi-site PROC MIXED validation workflow
text: Treatment time LS-means treatment differences generated without hand-written contrast rows.
code: library(lmmix)  fit <- lmm(   data = multicentre,   formula = Y ~ Drug * Time,   random = ~ 1 | Center,   repeated = ~ Time | Center:Drug:Subject,   structure = "ar1",   method = "REML",   ddf = "satterthwaite" )  fit #> Linear mixed model fit by REML #> Formula: `Y ~ Drug * Time` #> Random: `~1 | Center` #> Repeated: `~Time | Center:Drug:Subject` (AR1) #> Log-likelihood: -245.313 #> Convergence code: 0 VarCorr(fit) #> # A tibble: 3 × 5 #>   group    term        component estimate std.error #>   <chr>    <chr>       <chr>        <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 Center   (Intercept) var          5.17     5.73   #> 2 Residual ar1         var         10.7      2.14   #> 3 Residual ar1         cor          0.935    0.0169 anova(fit) #> Type III tests with model degrees of freedom #> # A tibble: 3 × 5 #>   term      num.df den.df statistic  p.value #>   <chr>      <dbl>  <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 Drug           2   46.4     11.4  9.23e- 5 #> 2 Time           2   68.5     59.3  1.16e-15 #> 3 Drug:Time      4   68.4      1.35 2.60e- 1 lsmeans(fit, ~Drug) #> # A tibble: 3 × 8 #>   Drug  estimate std.error    df statistic p.value conf.low conf.high #>   <fct>    <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 1         13.2      1.53  2.98      8.60 0.00339     8.27      18.0 #> 2 2         18.1      1.53  3.01     11.8  0.00128    13.3       23.0 #> 3 3         17.1      1.53  3.01     11.1  0.00154    12.2       21.9 lsmeans(fit, ~Time) #> # A tibble: 3 × 8 #>   Time  estimate std.error    df statistic p.value conf.low conf.high #>   <fct>    <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 1         15.0      1.40  2.08      10.7 0.00753     9.16      20.8 #> 2 2         15.7      1.40  2.09      11.2 0.00670     9.90      21.4 #> 3 3         17.7      1.40  2.12      12.6 0.00495    12.0       23.5 lsmeans(fit, pairwise ~ Drug) #>  #> ── Estimated marginal means ── #>  #> # A tibble: 3 × 8 #>   Drug  estimate std.error    df statistic p.value conf.low conf.high #>   <fct>    <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 1         13.2      1.53  2.98      8.60 0.00339     8.27      18.0 #> 2 2         18.1      1.53  3.01     11.8  0.00128    13.3       23.0 #> 3 3         17.1      1.53  3.01     11.1  0.00154    12.2       21.9 #> ── Pairwise contrasts ── #> # A tibble: 3 × 8 #>   contrast estimate std.error    df statistic   p.value conf.low conf.high #>   <chr>       <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 1 - 2       -4.99      1.10  46.1    -4.54  0.0000400    -7.20     -2.78 #> 2 1 - 3       -3.89      1.10  46.1    -3.54  0.000914     -6.11     -1.68 #> 3 2 - 3        1.10      1.10  46.9     0.993 0.326        -1.12      3.32

title: Multi-site PROC MIXED validation workflow
text: stored reference values correspond following PROC MIXED specification. RANDOM REPEATED statements represent two covariance sources, DDFM=SATTERTHWAITE requests row-specific denominator degrees freedom documented SAS Institute Inc. (2026). stored SAS reference values, levels oriented ascending data order, : two-sided p-value last contrast 0.0001; package computes 3.998e-05 rounding. remaining pairwise references :
code: proc mixed data=multicentre method=reml;   class Center Drug Subject Time;   model Y = Drug Time Drug*Time / ddfm=satterth solution;   random Center;   repeated Time / type=ar(1) subject=Subject(Center*Drug);   lsmeans Drug Time / diff; run;

title: Multi-site PROC MIXED validation workflow
text: benchmark degrees freedom retained precision shown PROC MIXED. Tests compare estimates standard errors absolute tolerance 1e-3, degrees freedom displayed precision. implementation also checked nlme, lmerTest, mmrm model classes packages overlap lmmix.

title: ML, REML, and Satterthwaite inference
text: covariance parameters η\eta, lmmix constructs V(η)=ZG(η)Z𝖳+R(η). V(\eta) = ZG(\eta)Z^\mathsf{T} + R(\eta). decomposition use structured residual covariance matrices follow general Gaussian mixed-model framework described Harville (1977) Pinheiro Bates (2000). Variances Cholesky diagonal elements represented log scale. AR(1) correlations use hyperbolic tangent. Compound symmetry uses scaled logistic transformation respects dimension-dependent lower bound. Toeplitz correlations obtained partial autocorrelations, unstructured matrices use Cholesky factor.

title: ML, REML, and Satterthwaite inference
text: given VV, generalized least-squares estimator β̂=(X𝖳V−1X)−1X𝖳V−1Y. \widehat\beta = (X^\mathsf{T}V^{-1}X)^{-1}X^\mathsf{T}V^{-1}Y. profiled negative ML log-likelihood 12{nlog(2π)+log|V|+(Y−Xβ̂)𝖳V−1(Y−Xβ̂)}. \frac{1}{2}\left\{ n\log(2\pi) + \log|V| + (Y-X\widehat\beta)^\mathsf{T}V^{-1}(Y-X\widehat\beta) \right\}. REML criterion adds log⁡|X𝖳V−1X|\log|X^\mathsf{T}V^{-1}X| replaces nn n−pn-p normalizing constant. REML originates Patterson Thompson (1971); direct likelihood derivations given Harville (1977) LaMotte (2007). Cholesky solves used instead explicit matrix inverses.

title: ML, REML, and Satterthwaite inference
text: contrast row LL, define q(η)=LVar⁡(β̂)L𝖳. q(\eta) = L\operatorname{Var}(\widehat\beta)L^\mathsf{T}. g=∂q/∂ηg = \partial q/\partial\eta Σ̂η\widehat\Sigma_\eta inverse observed-information matrix, implemented approximation Satterthwaite approximation (Satterthwaite 1946): ν=2q2g𝖳Σ̂ηg. \nu = \frac{2q^2}{g^\mathsf{T}\widehat\Sigma_\eta g}. gradient likelihood Hessian calculated numDeriv. multi-row hypothesis, covariance Lβ̂L\widehat\beta decomposed orthogonal one-dimensional contrasts combined Fai-Cornelius moment-matching formula (Fai Cornelius 1996).

title: ML, REML, and Satterthwaite inference
text: covariance estimation, empirical BLUP û=GZ𝖳V−1(Y−Xβ̂). \widehat u = GZ^\mathsf{T}V^{-1}(Y-X\widehat\beta). empirical best linear unbiased predictor follows mixed-model derivation Harville (1977).

title: Authors and Citation
text: Package Maintainer. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Maintainer P (2026). lmmix: Linear Mixed Models Correlated Residuals. R package version 0.1.0.
code: @Manual{,   title = {lmmix: Linear Mixed Models with Correlated Residuals},   author = {Package Maintainer},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0}, }

title: lmmix
text: lmmix fits Gaussian linear mixed models explicitly optimizing ML REML criterion V=ZGZ𝖳+R. V = ZGZ^\mathsf{T} + R. package designed models combine random effects correlated residuals. provides Satterthwaite denominator degrees freedom, type III tests, estimated marginal means, pairwise contrasts, broom methods, emmeans interoperability. package name lmmix; main fitting function lmm().

title: lmmix
text: Install package local checkout:
code: devtools::install("path/to/lmmix")

title: lmmix
text: included multicentre data reproduce multilocation repeated-measures example Milliken Johnson (2009, Section 28.3). contain 153 rows, including 28 missing responses. data-first interface also works base R pipe:
code: library(lmmix)  fit <- lmm(   data = multicentre,   formula = Y ~ Drug * Time,   random = ~ 1 | Center,   repeated = ~ Time | Center:Drug:Subject,   structure = "ar1",   method = "REML",   ddf = "satterthwaite" )  summary(fit) anova(fit) lsmeans(fit, pairwise ~ Drug) fit <- multicentre |>   lmm(     formula = Y ~ Drug * Time,     random = ~ 1 | Center,     repeated = ~ Time | Center:Drug:Subject,     structure = "ar1"   )

title: lmmix
text: residual structures : id: homogeneous independent residuals; cs: compound symmetry; ar1: first-order autoregression, including negative correlation; toep: positive-definite Toeplitz covariance parameterized partial autocorrelations; un: unstructured covariance parameterized Cholesky factor. Random intercepts random slopes use unstructured Cholesky-parameterized G matrix.

title: lmmix
text: Executable tests compare package nlme ML, REML, random intercepts, random slopes, compound symmetry, AR(1). marginal AR(1) model also compared mmrm. Satterthwaite tests compared lmerTest models overlap. multilocation validation tests compare fitted covariance parameters, fixed effects, type III tests, LS-means, pairwise differences, Satterthwaite degrees freedom stored SAS PROC MIXED benchmarks model specification. validation note documents theoretical sources, model specification, numerical tolerances.

title: lmmix
text: Patterson, H. D., Thompson, R. (1971). Recovery inter-block information block sizes unequal. Biometrika, 58(3), 545-554. https://doi.org/10.1093/biomet/58.3.545 Harville, D. . (1977). Maximum likelihood approaches variance component estimation related problems. Journal American Statistical Association, 72(358), 320-338. https://doi.org/10.1080/01621459.1977.10480998 LaMotte, L. R. (2007). direct derivation REML likelihood function. Statistical Papers, 48(2), 321-327. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00362-006-0335-6 Satterthwaite, F. E. (1946). approximate distribution estimates variance components. Biometrics Bulletin, 2(6), 110-114. https://doi.org/10.2307/3002019 Fai, . H.-T., Cornelius, P. L. (1996). Approximate F-tests multiple degree freedom hypotheses generalized least squares analyses unbalanced split-plot experiments. Journal Statistical Computation Simulation, 54(4), 363-378. https://doi.org/10.1080/00949659608811740 Searle, S. R., Speed, F. M., Milliken, G. . (1980). Population marginal means linear model: alternative least squares means. American Statistician, 34(4), 216-221. https://doi.org/10.1080/00031305.1980.10483031 Pinheiro, J. C., Bates, D. M. (2000). Mixed-Effects Models S S-PLUS. Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/b98882 Milliken, G. ., Johnson, D. E. (2009). Analysis Messy Data, Volume 1: Designed Experiments (2nd ed.), Section 28.3. Chapman Hall/CRC. https://doi.org/10.1201/EBK1584883340

what: Fit a Gaussian linear mixed model — lmm
title: Fit a Gaussian linear mixed model — lmm
text: lmm() explicitly evaluates profiled ML REML criterion V = Z G Z' + R. can combine random effects independent, compound-symmetric, AR(1), Toeplitz, unstructured residual covariance.

title: Fit a Gaussian linear mixed model — lmm
code: lmm(   data,   formula,   random = NULL,   repeated = NULL,   structure = c("id", "cs", "ar1", "toep", "un"),   method = c("REML", "ML"),   ddf = c("satterthwaite", "residual", "kenward-roger"),   control = lmm_control() )

title: Fit a Gaussian linear mixed model — lmm
text: data data frame tibble. Placing data first makes function pipe-friendly. formula two-sided fixed-effects formula. random Optional one-sided random-effects formula ~ 1 | subject ~ 1 + time | subject. repeated Optional one-sided repeated-measures formula ~ time | subject. structure Residual covariance structure: "id", "cs", "ar1", "toep", "un". method Estimation method: restricted maximum likelihood ("REML") maximum likelihood ("ML"). ddf Denominator degrees--freedom method. "satterthwaite" "residual" implemented. "kenward-roger" reserved future implementation. control object returned lmm_control().

title: Fit a Gaussian linear mixed model — lmm
text: object class lmm.

title: Fit a Gaussian linear mixed model — lmm
text: Harville, D. . (1977). Maximum likelihood approaches variance component estimation related problems. Journal American Statistical Association, 72(358), 320-338. https://doi.org/10.1080/01621459.1977.10480998 LaMotte, L. R. (2007). direct derivation REML likelihood function. Statistical Papers, 48(2), 321-327. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00362-006-0335-6 Pinheiro, J. C., Bates, D. M. (2000). Mixed-Effects Models S S-PLUS. Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/b98882

title: Fit a Gaussian linear mixed model — lmm
code: fit <- lmm(   data = nlme::Orthodont,   formula = distance ~ age + Sex,   random = ~ 1 | Subject ) summary(fit) #>  #> ── Linear mixed model ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Estimation: REML #> Denominator df: satterthwaite #> Residual covariance: ID #>  #> ── Fixed effects ── #>  #> # A tibble: 3 × 8 #>   term        estimate std.error statistic    df  p.value conf.low conf.high #>   <chr>          <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 (Intercept)   17.7      0.834      21.2   99.4 1.04e-38   16.1      19.4   #> 2 age            0.660    0.0616     10.7   80.0 3.95e-17    0.538     0.783 #> 3 SexFemale     -2.32     0.761      -3.05  25.0 5.38e- 3   -3.89     -0.753 #> ── Type III tests ── #>  #> # A tibble: 2 × 5 #>   term  num.df den.df statistic  p.value #>   <chr>  <dbl>  <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 age        1   80.0    115.   3.95e-17 #> 2 Sex        1   25.0      9.29 5.38e- 3 #> ── Covariance parameters ── #>  #> # A tibble: 2 × 5 #>   group    term        component estimate std.error #>   <chr>    <chr>       <chr>        <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 Subject  (Intercept) var           3.27     1.07  #> 2 Residual id          var           2.05     0.324 #> ── Information criteria ── #>  #>    logLik       AIC       BIC  deviance  #> -218.7563  447.5125  460.9232  437.5125

what: Control numerical optimization for lmm() — lmm_control
title: Control numerical optimization for lmm() — lmm_control
text: Control numerical optimization lmm()

title: Control numerical optimization for lmm() — lmm_control
code: lmm_control(   optimizer = c("nlminb", "optim"),   optim_method = "BFGS",   max_iter = 1000L,   rel_tol = 1e-08,   x_tol = 1e-08,   initial = NULL,   lower = -20,   upper = 20,   deriv_method = "Richardson" )

title: Control numerical optimization for lmm() — lmm_control
text: optimizer Optimizer use, either "nlminb" "optim". optim_method Method passed stats::optim() optimizer = "optim". max_iter Maximum number optimizer iterations. rel_tol Relative convergence tolerance. x_tol Parameter convergence tolerance stats::nlminb(). initial Optional numeric vector unconstrained starting values. lower, upper Bounds unconstrained covariance parameters. deriv_method Numerical differentiation method passed numDeriv.

title: Control numerical optimization for lmm() — lmm_control

what: lmmix: Linear mixed models with correlated residuals — lmmix-package
title: lmmix: Linear mixed models with correlated residuals — lmmix-package

title: lmmix 0.1.0
text: Added explicit profiled ML REML estimation Gaussian linear mixed models. Added independent, compound-symmetric, AR(1), Toeplitz, unstructured residual covariance matrices. Added random intercepts slopes unstructured covariance matrix. Added Satterthwaite fixed-effect inference, type III tests, estimated marginal means, pairwise contrasts. Added standard model, broom, emmeans methods. Added multilocation example Milliken Johnson (2009, Section 28.3) validation tests SAS PROC MIXED, nlme, lmerTest, mmrm. Added primary theoretical references REML, Satterthwaite inference, multi-degree--freedom tests, estimated marginal means.
